content:
The first expensive thing Codex (and we guess, any other similar software, like Claude) does in a large codebase is not writing code (and not even writing specs or plans), but some trivial task - finding out where anything lives.
That sounds mundane until you watch it happens again and again across sessions. Codex opens the repository, inspects the tree, reads the same base classes, checks DI, follows a few view models, learns the result wrapper, finds the network layer, and pieces together the app shell. Then the real task starts.
The next session often repeats the same work. And to be honest, with quietly unpredictable result…one day it starts and gets lucky to find the target functions, classes, models, etc. with the first try, but very next day it feels totally the opposite.
That was the problem Graphify solved for us. We use it as a persistent, graph-backed map that Codex can read before it spends a pile of context on raw files.
Graphify combines deterministic code analysis with selective AI-powered semantic extraction to build a unified knowledge graph of code, documentation, diagrams, and media. It keeps source code analysis local, applies AI only where semantics matter, and enables future queries against the graph instead of repeatedly scanning the original files. (you may read more at the GitHub)
Our projects are production mobile codebases with declarative UI, view models, use cases, repositories, a shared network wrapper, local managers, dependency injection, and global event infrastructure. The structure is not random. The difficulty comes from size, shared behavior, and the number of paths a change can touch.
For a developers it is not problem, to trace the sources, see how models related, how functions being called, etc. For Codex, that answer has a cost. Every repeated scan consumes tokens, time, and attention that could go into the actual change, enlarging the token context window and increasing the chance of hallucinations appear.
Search still has its place. Codex can grep for a class, jump to a method, or find a string fast enough. The problem starts when we need to find the whole chain instead of the some variable.
In a big codebase, Codex needs to know which pieces are central, who owns the behavior, and whether a small helper sits on some busy path through state, caching, managers, or app-level effects.
Without a graph, it learns that the slow way. It opens files, follows imports, checks usages, and builds a one-session mental map from scratch. That works once. It gets annoying when the same architecture has already been discovered three times before.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by authorGraphify changes the first move. Instead of starting with broad file reads, Codex can start with the graph report and targeted graph queries. It gets a compressed view of the codebase: communities, central nodes, relationships, inferred edges, and import-cycle checks. Then it opens the files that matter, making code reading more selective.
What Graphify Gives Codex
Graphify turns a repository into nodes and edges. Nodes can represent files, classes, functions, concepts, and communities. Edges represent containment, calls, references, imports, or inferred relationships.
In our workspace, the generated report gives Codex a quick structural summary before it touches implementation files. It shows that the repository has roughly two thousand nodes and several thousand relationships. It also shows no detected import cycles, which tells Codex to focus less on structural circularity and more on behavioral coupling.
The central-node list is especially useful for agent work. It points Codex toward shared abstractions with high connectivity: the result wrapper, the network wrapper, the base state holder, local state managers, and complex coordinators. Those are the places where a small edit can have a wide blast radius.
For a developer, that list is a helpful reminder (sometimes search in the generated graph.html may help even faster than the Android Studio search). For Codex, it is a routing table. It says: slow down here, inspect callers, preserve conventions, and avoid inventing a local patch when the shared abstraction already owns the behavior.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by authorHow Codex Uses It in Daily Work
When we ask Codex to work in an unfamiliar area, it can read the graph report, query the graph, identify the relevant community, and then open a bounded set of files. In our projects, a typical path might run from a composable event to a view model, through a use case, into a repository, and then into a network wrapper or local manager.
A search flow is a good example. Codex should not have to wander through the whole repository to understand what happens after `onSearchClick()`.
With Graphify, it can trace the path first: SearchScreen.kt sends the UI event, SearchViewModel.kt updates state and launches the work, SearchUseCase.kt owns the business step, SearchRepository.kt handles loading, cache, and result mapping, and ApiService.kt reaches the backend. If the flow also touches SessionManager.kt or UIEvent.kt, Codex sees those side paths before it edits anything.
That changes the way it reads code. Codex loads the files on the path and their direct dependencies, not every file that happens to contain the word “search.” It can tell whether the bug belongs to state handling, repository caching, one-time events, or shared session coordination.
This is where the graph pays for itself. Before changing SearchRepository.kt, Codex can check whether it is a narrow implementation detail or a shared path used by other screens. Before touching SearchViewModel.kt, it can see whether the behavior is local state or part of a larger event flow and tells Codex where the real risk is before the edit starts.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by authorThe biggest win
Persistence became the part we felt on the second and third session.
Large-codebase work almost never happens in one clean pass. We trace the issue, make a partial fix, stop for review, come back later, and find the next edge case. Without some saved understanding, Codex starts by paying the same entry fee again: read the same files, rebuild the same architecture map, rediscover the same ownership boundaries.
Graphify handles one side of that problem. It gives Codex a durable structural map outside the chat: GRAPH_REPORT.md for the shape of the project, graph.json for queries, and the graph itself for paths between concepts. A later session can recover the codebase layout before opening raw source files.
Intermediate persistence plans handle the other side. They capture what we were trying to do, which options we considered, what we already ruled out, and what still needs verification. That is task memory for long running edits or updates, but sure not a codebase memory.
Both matter. Graphify can tell Codex where the flow goes. A persistence plan can tell Codex why we were touching that flow in the first place. One does not make the other unnecessary.
Together, they changed follow-up work. Instead of starting with “learn the project again,” a later session can start with “use the graph to reopen this path, then continue from the last plan.” Less repeated discovery, fewer wasted tokens, and a much better chance that Codex resumes the actual work instead of replaying the previous hour.
